[Column 1]
TO MRS ZINA D. H. YOUNG.

MUSIC and light and warmth tonight
	Greet thee with language manifold,
Where first thine eyes beheld this spot
	A desert, desolate and old;
No roof of art shielded thy head,
	Nor music—save the wild birds voice,
Night sank and darkness hung around
	Yet even then thou did’st rejoice.

For then, a Prophet’s voice proclaimed
	It chosen ground, where peace and rest
Should guard his people till they grew
	In strength, with homes and power possessed
’Twas solitude, yet fear ne’er came
	To break their slumbers; 'round them rose
The harvest yield to bless their need,
	Where long had reigned but waste and snows.

Though unforgotten, kindred, homes,
	And sorrows knit in blood and tears,
Faith wrought its daily steadfast task
	And gathered strength with passing years.
Though famine came, God heard their prayers
	And white winged flocks in answer sped;
They stayed the foe, and Israel
	Gathered the manna and the bread.

Within tbeir lowly cabin walls
	The mothers sang their cradle songs,
The years sped on, and ere they knew,
	Their scanty numbers swelled to throngs,
And these outspreading, woke to life
	The sleeping valleys by each stream,
O’er mountain ranges far away,
	Their growth, their blessings, like a dream.

Fair Utah cannot hold the tide
	Of human life that God so sent,
Thou hast beheld them on the Isles,
	They dot the farther continent;
And to and fro God’s messengers
	Bear forth His Gospel and return,
For this is Zion; all the earth
	Her wond’rous story yet shall learn.

From ev’ry Saint that dwelleth here,
	From Saints that long this land to see,
This Day is wafted heavenward
	Remembrances and prayers for thee.
From weary march and roofless camp
	Unto the Temples famed and grand,
Thy feet trod first the barrenness
	And to His houses lent thy hand.

And thou hast gained that higher gift
	That worldly wisdom cannot teach,
Tongues of the vanished speak to thee,
	Thine inner sight, beyond doth reach.

[Column 2]
Thou hast been mother unto more
	Than thine own children, and the name
“Mother in Israel" is thine own
	And all who love thee share a claim.

Linked with this dispensation is
	Thy hist’ry from its earliest date,
In ev’ry Temple thou hast wrought
	Salvation for the small and great.
O, souls redeemed in other spheres!
	If thou hast knowledge of this day,
Join in our love and praise of her
	Who led from prison world thy way.

May mightiest love from higher, life
	Blend with our love and prayers below,
And heavenly power attend thy days
	While still on earth thy face we know;
Still drawing nearer to the source
	Thy listening soul learn holier love,
Till—passing—memory keeps thy name
	In Earth and Heaven, forever more.
AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
Bountiful, Jan. 22nd, 1896.


[Column 1]
ZINA D. H. YOUNG.

A BRIEF SKETCH OF HER LIFE AND
LABORS.
	ON the 31st day of January, yester-
day, Sister Zina D. H. Young reached
the age of seventy-five years. “Aunt
Zina,” as the Latter-day Saints love to call
her, was born January 31, 1821, in Water-
town, Jefferson county, New York, and
comes of goodly parentage. Her father
was William Huntington, her mother Zina
Baker, whose father was one of the first
physicians in New Hampshire. Her mater-
nal grandmother was Dorcas Dimock,
“descended from the noble family of Dim-
ocks whose representatives held the heredi-
tary knight-championship of England;
instance: (Sir Edward Dymock, Queen
Elizabeth’s champion).” Mrs Young’s father
served in the war of 1812; Samuel Hunting-
ton, one of the signers of the Declaration of
Independence, was the uncle of her grand-
father, Wm. Huntington, a revolutionary
soldier. The family were directly descend-
ed from Simon Huntington, the Puritan
emigrant, who sailed for America in 1633,
but died at sea leaving three sons and a
widow, who were registered in Roxbury,
Mass. Wm Huntington, the grandfather,
married Presendia Lathrop, and was one of
the first settlers in the Black River valley,
northern New York.
	The Hu[-]tingtons embraced the gospel at
Watertown, New York and Zina Diantha
was baptized by Patriarch Hyrum Smith
August 14, 1835, and soon after went to-
Kirtland with her father’s family. In Kirt-
land she received many blessings and mani-
festations, the gift of tongues, and of
interpretation; she heard an invisible choir
of angels singing in the Kirtland Temple,
and was present at the memorable Pentecost
when the spirit of God filled the Temple,
like a mighty rushing wind. Sister Zina
was a member of the Kirtland Temple choir.
	With her father’s family Sister Zina
passed through the terrible persecutions in
Missouri of which there is not time to tell.

[Column 2]
	Like many other exiles they succeeded in
reaching Commerce, afterwards called Nau-
voo, where the Saints suffered so much.
	At this place Sister Huntington, the mo-
ther, died, a martyr to suffering from the
mobbings and drivings.
	In Nauvoo Sister Zina became a member
of the first Relief Society organization, little
dreaming what would be in the future, and
how great the work would become. When
the Nauvoo Temple was ready for ordinan-
ces she received her blessings and endow-
ments therein.
	She left Nauvoo with the Saints in the
month of February, 1846, with the first
company, crossing the Mississippi river on
the ice. Arriving at Mt. Pisgah, where the
exiles stopped and made a resting place, her
father was taken sick and died. She re-
mained in Winter Quarters on the Missouri
river, until 1848, when she started for the
valley; beginning the journey in May,
reached the valley in September.
	When the Relief Society was established
in Utah, Sister Zina was chosen as treasurer
of the first society in the Eighteenth ward,
where she resided. Some years after, when
Sister Eliza R. Snow was called by Presi-
dent Brigham Young to assist in organiz-
ing in wards, Sister Zina was also called
by the President as Counselor to Sister Eliza,
and she was associated with her in the la-
bors of the Relief Society and traveled in its
interest during Sister Eliza’s lifetime.
	In the silk industry Sister Zina has been
indefatigable and zealous, laboring as no
other woman in this Territory has done, to
establish sericulture. she has raised co-
coons herself, and had charge of President
Young’s large cocoonery and mulberry
orchard. In June, 1876, when a silk
association was organized Sister Zina was
elected President, and she has traveled the
length and breadth of the Territory in its
interest.
	Sister Zina also took a course of medical
studies under Dr. Willard Richards in the
early days in the valley, and became familiar
with the practice among women, and has
given numerous lectures on the subject of
nursing, hygiene and other kindred topics.
	She has traveled as extensively among
the people and spoken as many times as any
other woman and perhaps even more. She
has a rare faculty of ministering to the sick
and sorrowing, and is often alluded to as
“Aunt Zina the Comforter;” having such
intense sympathy, added to the gift of heal-
ing, which she seems naturally to possess.
	Her presence in the sick room is always
soothing aud uplifting to the afflicted.
	Sister Zina is an inspirational impromptu
speaker; her speeches, in mass meetings a[-]d
and on various great occasions, have been
widely circulated by newspaper reporters.
	She holds her audience without an effort
by her natural eloquenoe.
	In 1879 she visited the Sandwich Islands.
During this trip she met many noted people.
The same fall she accompanied Sister Eliza
to St. George. There they labored in the
Temple, and among the sisters’organiza-
tions, held meetings, traveled over a thou-
sand miles, often camping out over night.


[Column 1]
In 1881 she visited relatives in New York
and Vermont and collected genealogical
records of her ancestry; and while away
often spoke in Sunday schools and temper-
ance meetings, and conversed with many
celebrated divines; attended the Woman’s
congress at Buffalo and a National Suffrage
Convention in New York city. Sister Zina
also assisted the Elders in organizing a
Relief Society at Cohoes, New York, dur-
ing that visit.
	In 1882, when the Deseret Hospital was
established, Sister Zina was chosen vice
president, and occupied the arduous position
for twelve years; the same year she attend-
ed the National Suffrage Convention in
Omaha, and visited relatives in St. Joseph,
Mo. At this convention she made the ac-
quaintance of several celebrated women,
May Wright Sewall, Phoebe Cousins, and
others, also Susan B. Anthony, whom she
had already met here on her first visit to
Utah in 1870, and afterwards in New
York. Sister Zina has continued her labors
among the people and in the house of the
Lord without ceasing year after year, and
thousands rise up and call her blessed. She
was for thirty years and even more, a dili-
gent Sunday school teacher.
	After the decease of Sister Eliza R. Snow
in December 1887, Sister Zina being her
first counselor, continued acting president
until April, 1888, when she was called by
President Wilford Woodruff and set apart
to preside over the Relief Society. In 1891,
the Relief Society became formally connect-
ed with the National Council of Women of
the United States, which entitles the presi-
dent to the position of vice-president in this
great council. In 1893, when the congress
of representative women from all the world
met in the Art Palace in Chicago at the
time of the World’s Fair, Mrs. Young was
present and had the privilege and the right
of taking part in the important proceedings.
She also held a meeting of the Relief Soci-
ety in one of the halls of the Art Palace,
which attracted much attention. At this
meeting Isabella Beecher Hooker, Elizabeth
Lisle Saxon and other celebrities were pre-
sent, and some of them requested the privi-
lege of speaking. On the Sunday follow-
ing at the services in the large hall, Mrs.
Young was seated upon the platform with
fourteen women ministers of different de-
nominations, conversing freely with them
after the service upon the principles of
our faith. She also attended the Congress of
Charities and Philanthropies, of which she is
vice-president. Mrs. Young’s labors have
been unceasing and though she has reached
such a great age, her usefulness is not dim-
nished, but increases as the field of her
labors widens out, and develops more and
more. Her entire time is given in the ser-
vice of the Lord, in the duties of her calling
in the Relief Society and to the ministra-
tions in the Temple, where she has been
called to preside over the sisters—a long and
a beautiful life, of which scarcely the bare
outlines are given here. Sister Zina is de-
cidedly, a woman of the people, beloved by
the Saints for her many excellent qualities,
her integrity to the Gospel and her abundant
charity.
	We have only spoken of Sister Zina here
as a public woman in the capacity of a
missionary laboring among the sisters, but
her home life has been equally exemplary
and successful. She is tender and motherly
in her nature, and though she has not had a
large family of her own (two sons and a

[Column 2]
daughter) she has several children who call
her mother, now grown to manhood and
womanhood, to whom she has given the
same loving attention as to her own, and
who love and reverence her in return as do
her own.
	Sister Zina became the wife of the Pro-
phet Joseph Smith in Nauvoo, and after his
death was married to Brigham Young. It
is impossible to enter into details in a news-
paper article. Volumes might be written
of Sister Zina—her name has gone far and
wide, and she is known everywhere for her
good works; no woman in the Church is
better known or more highly respected, and
not only at home but abroad, she is appro-
priately designated a mother in Isreal, which
she is in very deed. E. B. W.


IC 6			WOMAN’S EXPONENT


ANNIVERSARY PARTY.

	On the morning of Sept. 1st, 1895, the
Saints of Farr West and many of the
realitives and friends of our worthy Bishop
W. F. McEntire and his estimable wife
met to celebrate the sixtieth birthday, also
their fortieth wedding anniversary; the
ward clerk had the bishop very busily en-
gaged in looking over the ward records,
and his wife was busy preparing dinner as
she had invited her family and some friends
to take dinner with them. While this was
going on the crowd met and formed in line,
headed by the band, next the Relief Society
then relatives and friends and marched to
their residence, the band struck up a lively
air which aroused all in the house, the
Bishop opened the door to see what it
meant, threw up his hands and exclaimed,
“I’ll surrender.” After the shaking of
hands all proceeded to the hall, where two
tables were spread full length of the hall,
loaded with the bounties of life, then the
worthy couple were presented each with a
beautiful rocking chair; also a handsome
photograph album, and were then seated at
the head of the tables, after all were seated
Brother Peter Slater read an orginal poem
composed by himself very applicable for the
occasion.
	After lunch was over, a program was
rendered, first being music from the band,
Hon. D. H. Peery next addressed the as-
sembly, said he was happy to meet with so
many of his old friends and acquaintances
on this happy occasion, congratulated the
Bishop and his wife, spoke of his long ac-
quaintance with them, and of their many
good traits of character, thanked the sisters
for the good dinner, also thanked the band
for the excellent music Ex. Bishop, P.
G. Taylor of Harrisville, said God bless
you all, spoke many words of praise in be-
half of our bishop and wife and many
words of encouragement to all.
	Our bishop then responded, thanked all
very kindly, said we are all brothers and
sisters in very deed, "my whole heart and
soul is poured out toward all. May God
bless you,  amen.” Music by the band,
song by Lucinda Stephenson, “Shall we
gather at the river.” Recitation by Mrs.
Heber Holland, Brother Jacob Thomas
thanked all, and hoped we might have
many more snch occasions, it was indeed a
beautiful day as the sun was shining
brightly and all went off as bright and de-
lightful as the day.
	MARY A. STEPHENSON, Prest.
	MELINDA J. RAWSON, Sec.
Farr West Jan. 18, 1896.


IN THE INTEREST OF HEALTH.
From Woman's Tribune:– 
	HERE I send you another instance of
women being in advance of their brothers,
in care of the health of their families and the
public health.
	Several years since I was talking with a
business woman who, with her sisters, has
long conducted a millinery and also a dress-
making establishment. I had placed stress
upon the need of great care in the handling
of paper money, which passes through
"everybody’s" hands and wallets, and re-
marked that I frequently found it so objec-
tionable that I wrapped it in fine, clean
paper before placing it in my purse, and
added that it really needs disinfection. She
told me that for some years she had carbolic
acid in the till where the money is kept,
and thought they had really protected them-
selves from disease in this manner. Last
year I inquired of several bankers if they
used such precautions. Two of them re-
plied they had never done so, but the answer
from the last replying I send you.
	Dear Madam: Your note of the 19th inst.
is at hand. In reply, would say that we
have not made it a practice to disinfect
paper money or papers until recently. At
this time we consider the practice a protec-
tion to ourselves here in the bank and also
to the public at large. Respectfully yours,
	“CHARLES EMERSON, Ass’t Sec y.”
Cath. A. F. Stebbins, Detroit, Jan. 14, '96.


PERSONS sending twenty-cents to Harriet
Taylor Upton, at Hotel Arno, Washington,
D. C., will receive copies of the daily papers
for the week of the Convention.
MRS. DEBORAH REED, eighty-eight
yearsold,of Attleboro, Mass., voted “yes’’ on
the referendum. She writes to a grand-
niece in Oakland, Cal.: “It seems that
some women stand squarely in their own
light, and do not want their rights; so
those who do must wait awhile. Equal
suffrage will come sometime, though in the
not far distant future.”
THE social side of parliamentary life in
England, set forth by Mrs. Humphrey
Ward in her new novel in The Century,
“Sir George Tressady,” will be a revela-
tion to American readers. With the dinner
parties in Parliament Buildings and open-
air receptions on the Thames Embankment,
it presents a picturesque contrast to
American Congressional customs. 
QUEEN CHRISTINA of Spain has been
chosen arbitrator to settle a long standing
dispute over the boundary lines of Colum-
bia, Equador and Peru. This is the first
time in the history of the world that a wo-
man has been chosen to settle an inter-
national dispute. Her administration of
Spanish affairs has been characterized by
tact and statesman-like ability, controlled 
by high moral principle; and not the least
of her claims to admiration is the fact that
she has maintained peace in a country pro-
verbial for being in a state of constant un-
rest and turmoil. The peace, movement in
Europe has not yet evolved anything more
beautiful than the sentiment expressed by
Madame Potonie Pierre in the manifesto
sent by the French women to their sisters
of the German Peace Society: “The planet
is too small to permit of mankind destroy-
ing each other in any corner of it. — Wo-
man's voice.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
IN THE CITY OF THE DEAD.

A REVERIE.
I WALK in the darkening twilight,
	In the City of the Dead;
Whose streets so drear and silent,
	With autumn leaves are spread.
The wind is gently moaning,
	Dark clouds are overhead;
The sacred spot's deserted
	This City of the Dead.

I feel alone as I wander
	Tho' my loved sleep all around;
With trembling heart I view each spot,
	For this is holy ground.
I think of the trials and crosses
	That sadden the fairest life;
All now is peace and quiet:
	This is the end of strife.

So pensive I dream and wonder
	Where are the thousands now
Who sleep this sleep of silence,
	On the lonely hillside's brow?
Where are the broken-hearted
	Who wept on the lonely sod
When they laid away the loved ones
	When they bowed beneath the "Rod?"

Here in their turn they're resting,
	The young, the blithe, the gay,
And await the great redemption
	Of a Resurrection Day.
Here in God's great acre,
	Come the weary and distressed,
And in their turn are laid so low,
	Where none can mar their rest.

Here bitter tears are shed for them,
	We loved so long and dear;
The breast where laid our infant head,
	Our friends and kindred near.
Here manly youth and maiden fair,
	Are wrapped in death's embrace,
But the hearts that loved, for them cry yet,
	Time cannot grief efface.

Here too the babe whose days were few,
	Who came, then sighed and went,
A beauteous flower from Paradise
	To a sad, cold world was sent.
Just opening wide bright, wondering eyes,
	Where the light of angels shone;
Then sighing closed them gently down
	To gain a better home.

And here the aged come to rest,
	Here lay their bodies down;
For them no more is living sweet,
	Their hands reach for a crown.
They've lived a life of righteousness
	With prayer have sought their God,
And now at eve they go to rest,
	Beneath the grass grown sod.

Here in his prime the strong man lies,
	Here too the young wife's laid,
The cry of children oft goes up,
	From a mound that's newly made
Sad orphans roam the wide world o'er
	They cry for the loved in vain,
The throbbing hearts are silent now,
	They cannot beat again.

All ties are severed that ever bound;
	Death levels every one:
One tries in vain to bind them up,
	The work of man's undone.
The youth lives on, the bride is dead,
	The bride lives on the youth is gone;
The mother spent her life in vain,
	Here lies the little one.

And now to friendship drop a tear,
	For friends dwell here at last;
And like a day's forgetfulness,
	Seems the story of the past.

[Column 2]
O, aching heart. O, the throbbing brow,
	With weight of grief oppressed,
Come, view the spot where deep below
	The weary heart can rest.

Sleep on, sleep on the sleep of death,
	Ye folk of other days,
'Till Christ the Lord shall call you up
	To meet His Heavenly gaze.
O, hearts that grieve, O. hearts that mourn,
	The loss of loved ones dead,
Yet know "What life is to the living,"
	So "Death is to the dead."

They've crossed the river, the race they've won
	O, call them not again,
To a world of parting such as this
	Of sorrow and of pain.
For up and down the wide world o'er
	We hear the sad refrain,
Where is the world so fair and bright
	Where loved ones never part again?

A light thro' the long dark night is given,
	It anon flashes over the way,
O, pilgrim so weary of life's sad load,
	Press in 'tis Eternal Day—
There is rest for the weary ones at last
	Where never a tear is shed;
There's rest when the journey of life is past
	Among the silent dead.

All paths meet here, tho' wide they roam,
	Here is the place of rest,
And all sleep well the sleep of death,
	Like babe on mother's breast.
Thank God for the Gospel's joyful sound,
	Thank Him for a prophet's voice;
That tells of a world where the lost are found,
	Where dwell the loved of his choice.

Its golden spires are glittering high,
	Its music is wafting low;
"Come to Me, come to me, ye weary of earth
	For I have loved you so."
"I have made you a home of Jasper stones,
	And hung its walls with gold"—
Then the love of the great Eternal One,
	Will your trembling forms enfold.

"A gorgeous home I have made for you,
	In this glittering world on high,
Your loved ones are waiting to welcome you
	Come, come, they are drawing nigh.
Here the sun does not darken at the close of day,
	Here the breezes are coy and sweet"—
And the love of the Father is over all
	For here the loved ones meet.

Then heart be thou still, cry out no more!
	Thy jewels are shining there,
In gladness they tread the golden streets,
	And offer for thee a prayer.
Now hark! Through the gathering gloom
	Hear the angel voices tell,
"Daughter arise, thy cross take up,
	With thy loved ones all is well."
LYDIA D. ALDER
Oct 15th, 1895.


U. W. P. C.

	By special invitation the Utah Woman’s
Press Club held their December meeting at
the residence of Mrs. I. Cameron Brown.
President E. B. Wells presided in her usu-
ally dignified manner, calling the meeting
to order, and for invocation by Mrs. M. A.
Freeze. The members answered to roll
call by original sentiments.
	Mrs. Lizzie Wilcox read in her sweet im-
pressive manner an interesting paper on
Margaret Fuller, which was followed by a
song by Miss Pearl Russell.
	Mrs. C. Louise Boyden then gave in her
inimitable style a humorous recitation.


By special request our amiable hostess
Mrs. Brown, favored the club with a unique
and entertaining paper on the life and
works of Frances Burney.
	The names of Mrs. S. O. B. Cummings,
Mrs. Rachel S. Miller, and Dr. Ola R. Curtis
were presented for membership and the
vote carried unanimously.
	In behalf of the club an expression of
thanks was given by the President to Mrs.
Brown, who has upon different occasions
entertained the club so generously.
	Following the literary exercises dainty
refreshments were served in the most artis-
tic manner, “intermingled with a flow of
reason and a feast of wit,” for the musical
hum of voices kept time with the rattle of
china and the pleasant reunion closed with
many a cordial grasp of hands that never
met before, and all felt that heart came close
to heart and soul communed with soul.
E. R. SHIPP, Sec.


THE THIRD STAR.

	THE President’s Proclamation of the
Statehood of Utah issued January 4th, was
the occasion of a triumphal holiday all over
Utah and of a great demonstration in Salt
Lake City. The new Governor, Heber M.
Wells, did not forget to extend the hand of
greeting to the women who are now voters.
	There are no women in the United
States better fitted to exercise the right of
suffrage than the women of Utah. They
are accustomed to manage great enterprises
as witness their Relief Associations they
are thrifty, industrious, and simple and
sincere in tastes and habits. When voters
before they contributed to good local gov-
ernment. Since that time they have joined
hands with the women of the Suffrage As-
sociation, with the World’s Fair work and
in that union of all ties the National Coun-
cil, and in each capacity they have demon-
strated their earnestness and ability.
	Then too, there is something of poetical
justice that their rights should be accorded
them while as yet there are some to enjoy
it who helped to lay the foundations of their
prosperous commonwealth. In all that has
gone to making the desert-blossom like the
rose, the women of Utah have borne their
full share of workers, and that large share
of endurance and of sacrifice which in one
shape or another has always been the lot of
woman. Think of it, there are some still
left to exercise their tardily acquired right
who pushed their handcarts with their
babies and their bundles in them those
weary miles across the Great American
Deseret. The early Mormon women were
very largely of New England birth, and
they have gloried in having emulated the
exile and privations of the Pilgrims for the
same cause, namely freedom to worship ac-
cording to their conscience. Now that the
extraneous doctrine of polygamy is swept
away, the world will judge more fairly of
the Mormon citizens of Utah, upon the re-
sult of whose pioneer toils and courage
others are now building.
—Womans Tribune.


IN Colorado, last year, only one woman
held the office of county superintendent of
schools. Now there are twenty-three wo-
men superintendents. Yet some of our
remonstrants claim that women are less
likely to be chosen to school offices where
women vote.

	SALT LAKE CITY, FEBRUARY, 1, 1896.


[Column 1]
THREE QUARTERS OF A CENTURY.

	SOME of the sisters who have great love
for Sister Zina D. H. Young, and wishing
to manifest their appreciation of her labors
as a friend to humanity and a public ser-
vant, counseled together as to how they
could best express in a fitting manner the
esteem and respect of her associates on the
coming birthday which would complete her
seventy-fifth year—making the three quart-
ers of a century, which is a ripe age, and a
time, when one would no longer hesitate to
be called old, After careful thought it was
decided that as President of a great organi-
zation, the National Woman’s Relief So-
ciety, it might be appropriate to celebrate
by a meeting in every ward throughout the
organization. Accordingly this was decided
upon and letters were written to presiding
officers and some suggestions were made to
them as to the program of exercises.
	Meanwhile her children and intimate
friends planned a banquet in her honor to
be given on January 31, 1896, and the sis-
ters who are officers in the General and
Salt Lake Stake Boards of the several
Associations met and conferred together, as
to what should be done, and how to pro-
ceed. The matter was given serious atten-
tion and after many meetings of Commit-
tees the Hotel Templeton was agreed upon
as a suitable place and arrangements were
made to hold the banquet there. Commit-
tees were appointed to dispose of the work
and the affair was settled. Invitations were
circulated and every detail attended to as far
as possible. All these things were done with-
out the knowledge or consent of Sister Zina.
Souvenir cards were prepared, Sister Zina’s
picture in the left-hand corner—her name
and the dates 1821—1896—and a verse in-
scribed which spoke of her labors and vir-
tues.
	A saintly face, and a great mother heart,
	So rich in grace, and charily thou art, 
	That evermore our reverence shall be,
	A loving tribute to thy memory,
	E’en poets yet unborn shall sing thy fame,
	And future generations bless thy name.
	These cards represented the colors of the
Society which are in the banner, blue, white
and gold; cards white, edging and lettering
gold, ribbon with which it is tied blue. Mrs.
McCune with her usual generosity was on
hand with her carriage to convey eminent
guests. Sister Zina was brought from her
home into the parlors of the Templeton in-
to the midst of floral decorations and of

[Column 2] 
guests among whom were many very old
friends and distinguished visitors.
	About two hundred guests, relatives and
intimate friends of Mrs. Young were seated
at the banquet in the spacious dining room
of the Templeton, President Lorenzo Snow
of the Quorum of the Apostles offered the
benediction. Clive’s band discoursed the
choicest selections of music in the ante-
room during dinner, and after partaking of
the sumptuous repast, the company re-
paired to the parlors and the program of
exercises begun, Captain Willard Young
Master of Ceremonies; opening with music,
voluntary, on the piano by Miss Kate
Romney—song by Miss Carrol C. Cannon,
“Pilgrim’s Band of Hope” encore; Captain
Young’s address to his mother in recogni-
tion of the day, and her many labors and
teachings, which was both tender and elo-
quent—Singing led by Professor C. J.
Thomas and Maggie Hull, “O, my Father
thou that dwellest’’ etc. Response to toast,
“The Relief Society’’ by Mrs. Sarah M.
Kimball, giving the origin and organiza-
tion of that great and grand association.
Sister Bathsheba W. Smith followed con-
firming the statements made by Sister
Kimball and expressing her own views and
her delight on this happy occasion. Mrs.
Nellie C. Taylor read an original poem by
Emily H. Woodmansee after which more
music was given and a recitation by little
Zina Card—“Christ And The Little Ones”
song by Maggie Hull, poem by Augusta
J. Crocheron, read by Annie Wells Can-
non, telegrams of congratulations to Mrs.
Young from Ephraim, Nephi and Lehi in
behalf of the sisters of the society of these
places, were read. Speech by President
George Q. Cannon, in which he alluded to
the privileges of equality the sisters now
enjoyed and urged wisdom and caution in
the exercise of the franchise. Con-
gratulated Sister Zina on her labors, the
enjoyment of all her faculties and health
and strength at the great age, referred to
the many blessings the Saints enjoyed at
the present time.
	Response by the venerable President
Zina D. H. Young, and more music; speech
impromptu, Mrs. E. B. Wells and conver-
sation general among the guests. After-
wards Mrs. May B. Talmage recited “Only
A Girl,’’ very impressively rendered, and
there was music by Rhoda Mabel Young,
and songs in Hawaiian language by Elder
Jacob Gates and a speech by Elder Oliver
B. Huntington of Springville brother of
Mrs. Young; recitation “The New Church
Organ” by Mrs. Elizabeth McCune in her
inimitable style and humorous recitations
by Zebulun Jacobs grandson of Mrs. Young,
speech by George Chase of Centerville
and other exercises. The singing of
“Auld Lang Syne” completed the evening’s
entertainment. It was a very enjoyable
affair and a day never to be forgotten by
those who had the privilege of assembling
together and congratulating the lady in
whose honor the party was given.
	One thing to be regretted is that all the
very dear friends of Sister Young could not
have met in one place but there is no hall
large enough; and the Committees had to
decide on a certain number; in a party
given for one so universally beloved and
respected it would require a place as large
as the Theater or the Pavilion at Saltair.
	The entertainments given in the several
wards on the same day so far as we have
heard were very happy and enjoyable gather-
ings


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	ERRATA—In the January number of our
paper the last stanza and ninth line of the
poem ‘‘Dying Yet Living” the word
“freedom” should read “guerdon.”
	MRS. EMILY S. RICHARDS Vice Presi-
dent At Large of the State Suffrage As-
sociation and Mrs. Sarah A Boyer President
of Springville Suffrage Association, left the
City for Washington on the U. P. express
train Jan. 19, (Delegates) to attend the
National Woman Suffrage Convention at
the Capital. A number of Delegates were
nominated at an executive session of the
State Association on the 18th of December,
1895, and credentials furnished, but
on accouut of circumstances they could not
all go to the Convention. Among those who
were nominated besides the two already
mentioned were Lucy A. Clark, Farming-
ton, President Davis County Suffrage As-
sociation; Corinne M. Allen wife of the
Utah representative who is already, in
Washington, Augusta W. Grant, Ella W.
Hyde, Margaret A. Caine, Martha B.
Cannon wife of the Senator (Frank J.
Cannon,) Margaret B. Salisbury, Nellie
M. Little, Elizabeth McCune and Mary E.
Gilmer. Our knowledge of the Convention
is very meager, only what the dispatches
have given, and one or two personal tele-
grams we have received.


RELIEF SOCIETY REPORTS.

	THE Stake Secretaries of the Relief
Society in all the organized Stakes, and in
branches of the Society in this and other
countries where there is no Stake organized
should send in a statistical and financial
yearly report for 1895. At the last business
meeting of the Board of Directors of the
National Woman’s Relief Society, a quo-
rum being present, it was decided that the
annual report of the Society should be
made in February, therefore instead of
dating from the first day of January to the
last day of December inclusive, reports will
be hereafter from February to February of
each year. This year reports must date
from the first day of January 1895, or
otherwise a correct record could not be kept.
Secretaries should be careful and exact in
conforming to this official notice. All re-
ports should be forwarded to the office of
the Secretary, Constitution Building, Salt
Lake City, not later than the 20th of
February.
		EMMELINE B. WELLS, Secretary.


EXTRACTS FROM MRS. C. M.
ALLEN’S LETTER.

	I HAVE enjoyed attending the sessions of
the convention more than I can tell you.
	Many of these women of national
reputation are more than one has believed
them to be. I was greatly delighted with
Mrs. Catt. She is a woman of marvelous
power. I cannot speak in detail of all of
them. To do it would simply be repeat-
ing what you already know better than I
do.
	On Utah night the church was packed as
I never saw a house packed before.
	Hundreds were refused admission and
hundreds of women and men stood during
the long exercises.
	Miss Shaw’s speech was short not over
twelve or fourteen minutes, but the most


eloquent I have ever heard which is saying
a great deal. Mr. Cannon’s reply was in
his usual happy vein. Mr. Allen spoke
briefly and not apparently unacceptably.
Mrs. Boyer followed with a talk that
shewed her a woman of ability. Mrs.
Richards read her paper last. It was a
thoughtful and well prepared history of suf-
frage in Utah. I never saw her look so
handsome. Her dress was beautiful and
became her well. The rest of the evening
was given up to state reports and brief ad-
dresses. It was amusing to see that even
here in this great convention there were wo-
men who would run over their time, and go
on after the bell had rung.
Washington D. C. Jan. 24th, 1896.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE WASHINGTON CONVENTION.

	“THE twenty-eighth annual convention of 
the National-American W. S. A. opened
Thursday morning, Jan. 23, 1896, in the
Church of Our Father, Washington, D. C.
the president, Miss Anthony, in the chair.
In opening the meeting Miss Anthony said:
	This is the twenty-eighth annual suffrage
convention in Washington. The thought
that brought us here twenty-eight years
ago was that, if the National Constitution
could be invoked to protect black men in
the right to vote, the same great authority
could be evoked to protect women. The
question has been urged upon every Con-
gress since 1869. We asked at first for a
sixteenth amendment, enfranchising wo-
men; then for suffrage under the fourteenth
amendment; then when the Supreme Court
had decided that against us, we returned
to the sixteenth amendment, and have
pressed it ever since. In the present Con-
gress, Senator Hoar has introduced in the
Senate a joint resolution for a sixteenth
amendment, and it has been referred to the
Senate Committee on Woman Suffrage,
of which Senator Call, of Florida is chair-
man. A similar resolution has been pre-
sented in the House by Congressman North-
way, of Indiana, and has been referred to
the Judiciary Committee.
	You will notice that the seats of the dele-
gation from Utah are marked by a large
United States flag bearing three stars, a big
one and two smaller ones. The big star is
for Wyoming, because she stood alone for a
quarter of a century as the only State
where women voted. Colorado comes next,
because she is the first State where a ma-
jority of the men ever voted to grant women
equal rights. Then comes Utah; because
the men of Utah, in convention assembled,
in spite of the bad example of Congress,
which took the right away from the women
of Utah ten years ago,—the men of Utah,
having seen the good effects of woman
suffrage for years before that, voted by an
overwhelming majority to leave out the
little word "male" from their suffrage
clause. Next year, if I am here, I hope to
rejoice over California and Idaho, and the
next year over several more. We have
now three bricks laid down in the row. 
We have every reason for encouragement,
none for discouragement. One sign of
progress is that men in high places begin to 
talk back to us, instead of considering us
unworthy of notice. They read the Call
for our convention; and President Eliot, of
Harvard, does not like it that we quote
him. 
	Miss Anthony read the sentences from
President Eliot's article quoted in the Call,


and continued: Now, friends, you all know
I am a Quaker. We give thanks in silence
before each meal. I do not think the heart
of any one here has been fuller of silent
thankfulness than mine; but I should not
have remembered to have the meeting form-
ally opened with prayer if somebody had
not reminded me. Rev. Anna Shaw will
offer prayer.


[Column 1]
LADIES' SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of Semi-monthly Meeting held
in Fourteenth Ward Assembly Hall, Dec.
28th, 1895. President M. I. Horne pre-
siding. Singing, “How firm a foundation.”
Prayer by Sister Frances Smith. Singing,
“Guide Us O, Thou Great Jehovah.”
The minutes of the previous meeting were
read and accepted.
	President Horne said she felt very thank-
ful to be here with her sisters at the close of
another year, said there were enough here
to claim the blessing, “That where a few 
are met together in my name there will I be
also.” Many have come here today for a
blessing and felt that each one would re-
ceive the comfort she needed. Exhorted
her sisters if any one had feelings against
another not to let it have place in her
heart, but let them all die with the old
year and commence again anew with the
new year. Would like to have all her
sisters read, Christ's sermon on the Mount
often. Pray continually for the Spirit of
God for that led into all truth; do not let
your talents lie dormant but seek them out,
referred to President Cannon’s sermon on
the same subject.  Spoke of obedience of
children to parents also obedience to the
Gospel; said teach children self sacrifice;
good Latter-day Saints were self-sacrificing:
felt we were in a more dangerous condition
now than ever before and that there were
more temptations abroad for the young;
spoke of virtue, said she heard President
Cannon say he did not believe in long
courtships for young people. Spoke of the
Prophet Joseph Smith and his wonderful 
work all accomplished in fourteen years.
His life was not one of peace but of turmoil,
spoke of her association with him.  Bore a
strong testimony. Prayed the Lord to
bless us and help us to do better in the
coming year.
	Sister Cutler, said she wanted to bear
her testimony to the goodness of God: also
that He heard and answered prayer. Said
she went to the dedication of the Temple
fasting and everything she asked the Lord
for that day had been given to her four-
fold.
	Sister E. S. Taylor said she came here
today rather hungry for a blessing and felt
sure she would receive it; she never en-
joyed a meeting more in her life than she
did the conference here two weeks ago, did
not know why unless it was that she felt
free from all responsibility and could settle
down for pure enjoyment. She loved to
hear her sisters testify of their association
with the prophets; the dearest thing on
earth to her was her association with her
aged sisters, those who had done so much
good and had had so much experience, felt
she ought to be a great deal better than she
was; spoke very beautifully of her friend-
ship with Sisters Marinda N. Hyde and S.
M. Heywood who had passed behind the
vail and of their noble lives and the
example they set. Said we could not be
thankful enough for the Gospel, felt there

[Column 2]
was a great responsibility on the saints, al-
so that this was truly a time of trial for us,
knew Satan was not dead, felt that we
should ever be on the alert. Exhorted the
sisters to be charitable and each morning
before leaving our room ask God for His
Holy Spirit and for the help we need this
day: scan ourselves and never be above
asking one another’s forgivness if we have
done wrong, for then we could ask God to
forgive us: felt the coming year was full of
trials that we little knew of: we must be
humble and always try and say, “O God
Thy will, not mine, be done.” Prayed
that at the close of 1896 we might feel we
had served the Lord better than the year
before, closed by asking God to bless us all.
	Sister M. O. Brown felt glad of the
record she had made in the Temple this
year. When we could learn to listen to the
still small voice we were blest, we must
live near the Lord, said she never forgot
the sisters nor these meetings: hoped she
would be able to make a greater record in
the Temple this coming year than she had
in the last. Asked God’s blessing upon
us.
	Sister Elizabeth McCune said she had
not been identified with the Relief Society
until the last two months, had been more
with the young ladies: told of a, blessing
she had wherein it was prophecied she
should do a great Relief Society work.
Soon after this she was made Treasurer in
the Relief Society of the Seventeenth
Ward. The next Saturday she came to
these meetings and never enjoyed a meeting
so much. Felt she had missed a great deal
by not attending them before; thought it
would be a good idea to bring a pencil and
paper and jot down some of the words and
ideas of our dearly beloved sisters, Aunt
Zina, Aunt Bathsheba and Sister Horne,
for they were indeed gems and we would
not always have them with us: related a
dream she had; felt she had done better
in the last year than ever before, she felt
well and wanted to do do a great deal bet-
ter in the next. Sister Martha Barrow felt
she had always done the best she could,
had been in the church many years: God
had blessed her with good sons and
daughters. Her son had just come home
from a mission, felt very proud of him and
highly honored. Knew God would and
did bless the missionaries and kept them
from harm and temptation, bore a strong
testimony.
	A sister felt glad she was numbered with
the people of God, bore her testimony. 
	Sister Julia Sampson said the spirit
prompted her to add her testimony to the
others. She thanked her Heavenly Father
for the strength He had given her to enable
her to pass through her trials. Spoke of
the meeting one year ago, also of the sister
who spoke in tongues on that occasion
said there was much we could learn from
one another. We could not be happy in
the eternal world without our children. If
children go astray they will have to pay
the penalty. Hoped we would all have a
happy New Year and that God would give
us those things which would be best for
us.
	Sister Sophia Nuttall spoke of the effort
it was for her to speak, had been told in a
blessing that she should do this, felt this
was a trying time for the young and that it
would require all our strength to save
them: knew our labors would increase but
if we had patience and humility God


would give us the strength and help we
needed. Asked the Lord to bless us.
	Sister M. A. Hardy said she had made a
great effort to come here today, remembered
the meeting one year ago, also the sisters
who spoke in tongues. The desire of her
heart was to go to the Temple this winter;
prayed the Lord would open her way so she
could do so.
	Sister Brown spoke of the goodness of
the Lord to her, said each year she tried to
do better than the last, bore a faithful
testimony.
	Sister E. J. Stevenson felt thankful to 
see some new faces today: said we should
treasure the gems spoken by our aged 
sisters also their testimonies and 
recollections of the Prophet. We should be
a very thankful people, she had done the 
best she could in the past year but wanted 
to try and improve: said the fathers should
do their part in teaching and training the
children, thought parents should never
have differences before their children.
Prayed God to bless us all in the coming
year.
	Sister Jane James said she had lived in
the Prophet’s family: could not express
what a great man he was; she had many
trials and asked the sisters all to pray for
her. 
	Sister Horne made some closing remarks. 
The fathers could not come down to the
capacity of the children as the mothers
could: what children learned in their youth
they always remembered. When they
come home from Sunday School and
Primary, parents should ask them what
they have learned. Prayed we might al-
ways have the Spirit of the Lord to help
us bear our trials. Singing, “Lord dis-
miss us,” etc. Benediction by Sister J.
C.Howe.
			ZINA HYDE, Ass’t Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1] 
REJOICING AT HUNTSVILLE.

Editor Exponent:
	Dear Sister:—Feeling a desire to let you
know that we live in the tops of these
beautiful mountains, where the Lord said
his people should dwell, and can say that
we are from all nations, trying to learn of
his ways, and walk in his paths, building
up the waste places for our posterity to
dwell upon, of which you will see from a
synopsis of the celebration of the day of
days for this our beautiful Territory’s free-
dom. Statehood, we do appreciate this
grand privilege, also woman’s equality, to
advance in all that is progressive, as woman
should in order to bring forth those noble
spirits which it is said should be reserved
for these days.
	Our little valley was awakened by the
ringing of bells, our beautiful Meeting
House opened for exercises at 10 o’clock,
Jan. 6th.
	Some of our best speakers made short
speeches for the occasion, the brass band
was in attendance. At noon, the Relief
Society had prepared dinner for those who
took part in the formal opening of a fine
two story brick building, erected for the use
and benefit of all Relief Society work, also
the Young Ladies who have taken a very
prominent part in raising means to assist in
the great struggle of building and our dear
little primary childrens, nickles must not be
forgotten.
	At 2 o’clock the brass band made the

[Column 2] 
house ring with the best music of Hunts-
ville.
	After which meeting was called to order
by the chairman A. J. Anderson, music,
prayer by Counselor S. L. Peterson. Sing-
ing by the ward choir, after which a poem
composed by Sister Emma Peterson on
Statehood and the labors of the sisters, was
well rendered by her daughter Olivia.
Music by the guitar club. A speech was
then given by Lona Schada, on the labors
of the trustees expressing thankfulness for
the blessings of statehood and the privileges
of this beautiful house. A speech by Sister
Emma Burrows, she was glad to see this
day in celebrating the opening of this hall
for the benefit of all charitable purposes,
“for the poor ye shall always have,’’ said the
Savior; she never had had one doubt in re-
gard to the building of this house for she
fully realized it was all done under the in-
fluence and direction of the spirit, and
nothing but union of effort could have ac-
complished this work with so little means,
refered to the time of the laying of the
corner stone, by Apostle F. D. Richards the
prayer then offered, had been fulfilled, to
the satisfaction of those who were deeply
interested, namely the board of directors
and building committee; space will not per-
mit to tell of the unceasing labors of this
noble band of workers. Music and song by
the choir, after which President Tracy said
her heart was full of gratitude to God and
the brethren and sisters for this day. We
have labored so long who can doubt the
hand of God, scarcely any means to start
with, but here a little and there a little com-
bined labor under wise management and
we are here enjoying this day of freedom.
Music by the martial band. Speeches by
the committee of ladies and brethren. A
speech by Sister Hawkins, she was thank-
ful for this day and manifested her loyalty
and patriotism by proposing that three
cheers be offered in honor of the day, which
were given.
	Song by the young ladies; we trust at
the dedication of this building justice can
be done to all in the near future. Some
little expenses yet hang over it, success to
all such enterprises, all accomplished by
home made work.
	We remain your fellow laborers in the
Gospel.
			ELIZA A. TRACY, Prest.
			EMMA BURROWS, Secy.
Jan. 6, 1896.


R. S. REPORTS. 
MARICOPA STAKE. 
	MINUTES of Relief Society Conference
held at Mesa, Arizona, Dec. 20, 1895.
President Mabel Ann Hakes presiding.
Stake President Collin R. Hakes, Henry
C. Rogers, and other brethren present beside
a goodly number of the society.
President Alice Richens, Mesa Ward,
Louisa Harper,Lehi Ward, Ann Klienman,
Alma Ward, Esther Openshaw, Nephi
Ward, and Sister Sarah Tiffany of Papago
Ward, gave encouraging reports from these
respective wards. All seemed to be doing
as well as usual, although there seemed to
be a faithful few who had to do the princi-
pal part of the work of the respective
society, but upon the whole there was a
general good feeling and we had a good
meeting.
	Sisters Brimhall, Emma Rogers and
Sister Bailey bore their testimony and ex-
pressed joy and pride, that they were num-
bered with the Latter-day Saints.
	Sister M. A. Hakes, Sister Lizzie A.
Macdonald had visited all the wards during
the last three months, the other counselor,
S. A. Robson had not been able to go on ac-
count of her failing health. They found
everything working well and the sisters in
the various branches doing a good work in
caring for the sick and helping where
help was needed. Sister Lizzie A.
MacDonald gave some timely exhortations
as well as good advice to the Saints in gen-
eral, and mothers in particular.
	Brother Henry C. Rogers spoke for a
short time on the importance of attending
our meetings, as well as all other duties de-
volving upon a real live Latter-day Saint.
	President Collin R. Hakes was altogether
proud of the Saints of Maricopa Stake.
Spoke a short time on the duties of those
who had accepted positions of responsibility
in the Church, and the order of the Gospel.
Annual reports approved, also the minutes
of previous meeting by Bishop Eli Open-
shaw, conference was adjourned for three
months.	F. E. ROBSON, Sec.
SIXTH WARD.
	MINUTES of monthly-meeting held in
the Sixth Ward Assembly Room, Jan. 2nd,
1896. President West presiding. Present
Counselor Ellen R. Watson. There were
also present Bishop Jas. C. Watson, Coun-
selor Jesse West, M. Isabella Horne, Stake
President. Counselor Annie T. Hyde and
Clara C. Cannon. Also Brothers Jas,
Latham and Pugh. Singing, ‘’How firm a
foundation.” Minutes of previous meet-
ing read and approved.
	President West expressed her pleasure in
having President Horne and Counselors
with us this afternoon. Owing to the de-
mise of our late Counselor, Sister Britton,
Sister Janet Cochrane had been elected in
our last meeting to fill the vacancy, being
absent no further steps were taken at that
time; President West had spoken personally
with her later, owing to her advanced age
and poor health, she declined the position.
it was now in order to choose a Counselor.
Would like to hear from the Bishop on this
subject.
	Bishop Watson explained Sister Coch-
rane’s circumstances, said while it is our
right to call, we have also the right to ex-
cuse, Sister Cochrane’s excuse was a
righteous one, he was satisfied she did not
desire to shirk any duty. Spoke of her
faithfulness, her integrity, she felt in her
declining years unable to fill this position.
Spoke of President West’s long sickness,
her faithfulness. The good work the
society were engaged in.
	President West made choice of Sister El-
len Watson first Counselor, Sister Alice
Butterworth, second Counselor. On motion
of Bishop Watson, Counselor Ellen R.
Watson was elected first Counselor to
President West, carried by unanimous vote.
On motion of Sister Condie, Sister Alice
Butterworth was elected second Counselor.
carried unanimously.
Counselor Ellen R. Watson was set apart
by Bishop Jas. C. Watson.
Counselor Alice Butterworth by Coun-
selor Jesse West.
President Horne, "my sisters I am
pleased to meet with you.” Wished all a
Happy New Year, peace, health and pros-
perity. Spoke of the duties of the Presi-


dent, the order of the Relief Society, the
society should be in harmony with the
Bishopic of the wards. “I was connected
with the first Relief Society organized by
the Prophet Joseph. Heard the gospel for
the first time, three weeks after my mar-
riage with Brother Home. In shaking
hands with the Prophet Joseph, felt such
an influence that it thrilled my body like
electricity. Knew he was a Prophet of
the Lord. Had never lost the spirit of the
gospel from that time. Gave a brief sketch
of her life. Felt to prize and honor the
associations and acquaintance dearly. “The
adversary is abroad on the earth, we need
to be wise. Read the Book of Mormon,
Doctrine and Covenants and the Bible, those
who study these works have no time for
fault finding." Spoke of some, who were
connecting themselves with Christian
Science. Satan has great power on the
earth, we should watch and pray, that our
minds may not be darkened; be careful how
we speak of the Lord’s anointed. Spoke
of the power Satan exercised over the
messenger sent to Daniel in answer to his
prayer, by obstructing his path and en-
gaging in combat, how the Heavenly Mes-
senger who overcame him, also explained
to Daniel why he had been delayed. Spoke
of the prophecy of the Prophet Joseph on
the signs of the times. He said when the
Russian Bear puts his paw on the British
Lion be sure the end is not far off. Presi-
dent Horne said I am pleased with your
choice of your Counselors Alice Butter-
worth whom I have been acquainted with
for a number of years. Spoke of her
financial abilities, her faithfulness in the
gospel. Spoke of Sister Cochrane’s
integrity to the truth. Spoke of the
responsibility of the teachers. The Relief
Society were not to assist the poor alone;
but also to edify and instruct each other
and save souls. None but pure chaste wo-
men should be members; gave much good
instructions, especially in regard to storing
of grain. Closed her remarks by urging
the sisters to be diligent in discharging
their duties.
	Counselor Annie T. Hyde “I have been
pleased with the remarks of Sister Horne,
she always tells us something good." Desired
to assist in Zion’s cause. Regretted that so
few were here today, many did not realize
the importance of the Relief Society, both
old and young women should be members.
The Prophet Joseph was inspired to
organize the sisters. Emma Smith was
called to the office of President by revela-
tion. Joseph in giving instructions in the
third meeting, said none but women of good
repute should become members. The work
is not to relieve the poor alone, but to ex-
hort and expound the scriptures. Urged
the teachers to invite and encourage the
sisters to come to meeting. Did not know
how any could absent themselves from
meeting; the spirit of God could not dwell
with such. We need all the instructions
we can receive. Many blessings are
promised to mothers, if we are faithful we
can administer to our children, call on the
Lord to bless them,and to heal them if they
are sick. These are great and glorious
privileges for mothers to enjoy, the Priest-
hood of God is on the earth today, it is
patterned after the order of Enoch and
Paul.
	President Horne related an incident
where her child was healed throngh the
administration of Mother Smith, the
Prophet Joseph’s mother.
	Counselor Clara C. Cannon regretted that
the sisters were not more anxious to be con-
nected with the Relief Society, these as-
sociations were not only a benefit but a
great blessing to woman. Advised the
teachers to labor with them. Tell them of
the blessings to be derived from the good
instructions given there, how they would
help to lighten their labors at home. The
Prophet Joseph said every virtuous woman
should belong the to Relief Society. I have
heard the remark, that old women have
nothing to talk about, in this they make a
grand mistake. We can talk to each other
of the gospel, its principles never grow old,
we can learn something new every day
from it. My sisters, let us educate our-
selves! And know our true position, no
one can give.us their testimony, we have to
earn our own salvation, to know the truth
for ourselves; our conscience will tell us
when we do wrong. President Horne told
us of the power of Satan we cannot afford
to be careless. Read good books, be hum-
ble and prayerful. Seek to know the truth
for yourselves, that we may be faithful to
the end."
	Sister Alice Butterworth said “my sisters
I will try to fill the position assigned me,
by the assistance of my Heavenly Father,
had received many blessings through faith
and prayer.”
	Sister Ellen R. Watson said, “In accept-
ing the position I am called to occupy, my
only desire is to do good, to assist the poor,
to help the sick and afflicted, and perform
my duties.” Asked God to bless this
society.
	Sister Margaret Latham was set apart as
first Counselor to President Hunter of
Primary Association by Brother John
Latham. Sister Elizabeth E. Watson as
s econd Counselor by Bishop Jas. C. Watson.
Bishop Watson made closing remarks,
thanked President Horne and Counselors
for their visit. Exhorted the sisters to be
faithful in the discharge of theirduties.
	Adjourned for one month. Benediction
by Counselor Jesse West.
	After close of meeting. President Horne
and Counselors with Bishop Watson, Coun-
selor Jesse West and officers of Relief
Society repaired to President West’s where
a bounteous luncheon awaited them, pre-
pared by her daughters. After a pleasant
time and social chat, all returned to their
homes feeling blest and benefited by the
good instructions given.
ELIZABETH W. CONDIE.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
NOTES AND NEWS.

	IN the U. S. Senate, Hon. George F.
Hoar has presented a joint resolution for a
Constitutional Amendment admitting wo-
men to suffrage.
	MRS. CARRIE CHAPMAN-CATT has clear-
ed $140 for the National Organization
Committee by her Woman Suffrage Calen-
dars, which found a ready sale. We con-
gratulate her.
	LADY HENRY SOMERSET was the victim
of an attempted compliment at a recent
temperance meeting in London. She was
absent through illness. The lady who
took her place made this kindly but unex-
pected explanation: "Dear Lady Henry has
been overworked, and we must, of course,
be careful not to kill the goose that lays the
golden eggs."

[Column 2]
	IN Wyoming the State superintendent of
instruction, M1ss Estelle Reel, is conducting
her office with such skill and winning such
golden opinions that her nomination for
Governor of Wyoming in 1896 is being
seriously considered.
	MISS LAURENCE TADEMA, the daughter
of Alma-Tadema, has written one fairly
successful book, "The Crucifix." She has
just completed a translation of Maaterlinck’s
plays, for which she has written a preface
that is said to possess exceptional literary
merit. Miss Tadema is not yet twenty-five
years of age, and is an artist as well as a
writer.
	THE Woman’s Political Equality Club of
Rochester. N. Y., held a "Lucretia Mott"
anniversary social, at the residence of Dr.
E. M. Moore, on the evening of Jan. 16.
An interesting biographical sketch of the
"Saintly Lucretia" was read by Mrs. Jean
Brooks Greenleaf. Personal reminiscences
were given by Dr. E. M. Moore, who is a
nephew of James Mott, and by Miss Susan
B. Anthony.
	MRS. ELLEN M. HENROTIN’ president of
the General Federation of Women’s Clubs,
has issued a circular letter to all the club
presidents, asking them to bring forward
the consideration of a peace movement in
Europe and in this country. The ultimate
hope is to have a convention of the Chris-
tian powers in 1900, with a view to estab-
lishing an international court of arbitration.
	Many of the club presidents have already
acted in accordance with the letter.
	MRS. MARY A. LIVERMORE, recently 
entertained the Bostonian Club with rem-
niscences of "Boston Sixty Years Ago."
Her home was on Salem Street, near the
corner of Bennett Street—the court end of
Boston .Mrs. Livermore’s earliest recollec-
tion of an important event is the visit of
Lafayette, when the roads between Boston
and Worcester were gaily decorated, and all
the children in town dressed up in honor of
the event.
	A LARGE and brilliant assembly greeted
Miss Anthony and Mrs. Upton at the home
of the editor of the TRIBUNE last Saturday.
	There were Senators and members, with
their wives, the gifted ladies from National
Park Seminary, and a large number of the
prominent women of the District, many of
them with their husbands. Miss Anthony
had only been in the city a few hours, but
did not seem fatigued with her journey and
was in her usual health and spirits.—Ex.
	MISS HELEN M. WINSLOW, of this city,
who made a prolonged visit at the Atlanta,
(Ga.) Exposition, found much there that is
interesting and encouraging in relation to
the work and progress of women. In a let-
ter to the Boston Transcript she writes ap
preciatively of the women who composed
the Women’s Board of Managers, and re-
counts some of the many difficulties they
bravely overcame. She says significantly:
"While the Exposition itself comes out be-
hind in money matters, the Women’s
Board, who built and managed the Wo-
man’s Building, unaided and unadvised by
men, came out with about $4,000 in their
treasury."—Ex.
	MISS Clara Barton has sailed for England,
en route to Armenia, with the prayers and
sympathy of the American people for her
safety and success. It is a difficult and
delicate task to invade the domain of a


monarch to assist a province which has as-
pirations for political as well as religious
liberty, and we must not ignore the fact that
the persecutions have a political and not a
religious basis; hence the Sultan’s objection
to his rebellious subjects being assisted by
money raised at meetings called to denounce
the government. But Miss Barton will
make the humanitarian side prominent and
will never step on a Moslem to aid a Chris-
tian. She will assuredly secure not only
the permission but the co-operation of the
Sublime Porte.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
CONFERENCE N. A. W. S. A.

	Although my mind is simply teeming
with the work of this last year I think I
have told you enough, but the Colorado
women are in politics and they mean to stay
and the men like to have them although
some of them don’t, but they are not the
best men I assure you, and we prize the
ballot more and more. It has been said
that giving woman the ballot will simply
double the vote, that all women will vote
just as their husbands do or if they don’t
they will fight. I don’t vote as my hus-
band does. This is the twentieth year of
our married life and while he has been a
Republican and I have been a Democrat it
has not created any dissension between us;
he is perfectly delighted to hear my views,
it is variety that gives the spice to life, and
gives self respect to each of them. Yes,
the Democratic wife of a Republican hus-
band went all about the State and stumped
the State for the party to which her hus-
band was opposed. So I think that speaks
well for the husband. Miss Shaw is going
to speak to you now. I give to you women
of Utah the right hand of fellowship from
the women of Colorado, and I assure you
that as the days go by you will find the suf-
frage conferred upon you the most beautiful,
the most delightful and the most gratifying,
that your children will look upon you with
a great deal more respect because they will
realize that you have the same rights that
their fathers have, and I hope that our wo-
men will not vote just exactly as their hus
bands do. You know the children of a
Democratic mother and a Republican father
never can grow up thinking that all the
political wisdom in the world is found in
one particular party—they will, realize that 
it is divided up pretty equally, and honest
men and honest women may be divided in
politics if they will only remain loyal to
the declaration of Independence, loyal to
the stars and stripes and loyal to the Con-
stitution of the United States and the
magnificent expression of the power that
makes right and justice, which is embodied
in this glorious democratic—republican
government. (Applause.) 
	Miss Anthony: I will introduce to you
the Vice-president at large of a still larger 
Association than our Suffrage Association
and that is the Vice-president at large of
the National Council of Women of the
United States which has held its second
triennial council in the City of Washing-
ton and which three years from now will
hold its third triennial council and that
council consists not only of the Suffrage
Society but it consists of nineteen other
national organizations of this country; wo-
men organized for one and another great 
purpose, and as you have always heard it
said that you have never heard of any class
of women organizing for any other than a

[Column 2]
good purpose and I am proud always to
present as my first Lieutenant Miss Anna
Shaw. (Applause.)
REMARKS BY MISS ANNA SHAW.
	You know whenever women undertake
to do anything everybody has not always
wanted to do the first thing they say is, we
are getting out of our sphere. We have
heard that so long that some of us are not
the least bit frightened by it. Then if that
doesn’t frighten us to death they shout
“You are strong minded!” And we come
to feel since there are only two classes of
people the strong and the weak minded
people, we prefer to be classed with 
the strong minded (laughter.) Then the next
shot that is made is “You are a set of
Infidels!” If there is nothing else that
will kill you that will sure. Miss Anthony
during the New York campaign wanted to
impress upon the people that we were not,
and to make it strong she always brought
me up as a type of a strong orthodox back-
ing up the movement; so one day she
wanted to make a good impression and she
said “Now I have the pleasure of introduc-
ing to you an ordained minister and an or-
dained minister of the Methodist Church,
Rev. Anna Shaw who is my right bower,
(applause) and you can fancy the impres-
sion she gave on that orthodox community,
and the only person in that orthodox com-
munity who didn't know what a right 
bower was, was Miss Anthony (applause)
(laughter) all the rest of us knew just what
it was. Now my friends I hardly know
what to say because I want to fire a lot of
hot shot at you to night. In the old state
where I live we won't come into the Union
until the whole kite comes in and we will
come in at the tail end, so it seems like
talking to a person who is already free and
there is no possible hope of my being free
unless I come to Utah (applause) if it was
not that I like to be a missionary and come
here, but you don’t need missionaries here,
I want to go to the heathen (applause.) but
I was very much interested this afternoon
at what one of your gentlemen said and it
is so funny that the opposition are so sincere
and yet they don’t know just what they are
saying. This gentleman in his polite way
said he had such a high opinion of women
that he didn’t want to drag us down on a 
level with men on election day because
politics are so dirty and I thought the only
thing that is dirty about politics is the men,
(laughter and applause) and since we have
to live with these men 364 days in the year
why can’t they let us risk it the 365 days;
and if it is such a dirty place who can
handle a mop like a woman. (laughter)
When the Mayor of New York at one time
suggested the appointing of women on the
Board of Street Commissioners the Sun a
conservative old paper said to the Mayor
of New York. “Put your women on the
Board of Street Commissioners; who can
handle a broom so well as they.” And if
politics are dirty it does seem that it is time
they were cleaned up and they have got to
be cleaned up and we have got to come and 
help you. See how good we are. See how
kind we are! We were made to be your
helpmates. You are undertaking to run
this thing without us and you cannot ex-
pect to get out of it. I have always wanted
to see that and it is so wonderful that you
men want to keep it all to yourselves and
yet it is so safe. I cannot see why a decent
man is not as decent as a decent woman,

[Column 3]
and why a clean man is not just as clean as
a clean woman and why a moral man is not
just as moral as a moral woman, and I can’t
imagine how any decent man wants to get
down into any kind of a ruin. I have so
much respect for men that they have no
right to be asked to go anywhere where a
decent woman can’t go. I am getting tired
of this filthy pool of politics and strong-
minded women and all the rest of it. Why
is it a sort of a bugaboo to make them keep
still as the strong man will tell them. I
heard a woman on the train tell her children
if they didn’t behave that the Conductor
would punch a hole in their ears; I have
heard them tell lies to them, and then won-
der at Providence. But these people are
always telling stories to people to make
them keep still and scare them into good
behavior. Now Gentlemen, this dirty pool
of politics won’t scare us; we don’t believe
it and in the second place we are going to
stand right by you, we never have forsaken
you and we are not going to forsake you
now; and we will get out of our sphere. Is
it not wonderful that it is so easy for a wo-
man to get out of her sphere? I have been
out of my sphere for 25 years and I have
been exceedingly comfortable and a great
deal more so than in it and all these 25 years
I have been looking around to find a man
out of his sphere and I never yet found one
and I wondered why it was so easy for a
woman to get out of her sphere until I had
at last discovered that a man hadn’t any
sphere, to get out of. (Applause and laugh-
ter) But there is one thing true and that
is if you don’t get out of your sphere you
get into ours. (Laughter.)
	Not very long ago a good Methodist par-
son introduced me to a congregation and he
said “The only objection I have to you
women is that you push yourselves into the
kind of work that a man used to be engaged
in and what will become of us poor preach-
ers by and by,” and I said: “I think there
are a great many men preaching who would
to better at some other kind of business
and let women take the place and a good
thing it would be for the poor congregation
and an excellent thing for the preacher,”
and I said the difficulty with you is you
have been watching us so that you have
forgotten to watch yourselves. Years ago
there were only seven kinds of employment
and now there are more than seven hundred
kinds of employment in the United States in
which a woman can earn a livelihood; but
in the seven occupations in, which women
were engaged there are very few women to
be found to day, the men have taken them
up altogether.
